old_claim.note: Precision Machine Tools provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Precision machine tools and gauges enabled repeatable components to be made to tolerances that fit across assemblies.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica defines interchangeable parts as identical components and says mass production came through development of the machine-tool industry, with precision equipment producing large numbers of identical parts.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties industrial interchangeable-part production to precision equipment and the machine-tool industry.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The precision_machine_tools node is scoped to lathes, milling machines, boring machines, gauges, and related tools, matching the source's machinery and precision-equipment substrate.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the precision_machine_tools edge remains enabling but now has direct textbook evidence and a higher confidence.
would_reject_if: A stronger source showed industrial interchangeable parts were achieved without repeatable precision equipment or gauges.
The precision_machine_tools node were narrowed away from lathes, milling machines, gauges, and repeatable part production.
The interchangeable_parts node were rescoped to a preindustrial example that does not involve industrial machine tools.
short_reason: Britannica directly ties interchangeable parts to precision equipment and machine-tool development.
